ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general sushi class; no salmon-specific nigiri subclass exists. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers sushi as a category; no salmon nigiri-specific code exists. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for salmon sushi or nigiri sushi as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of salmon sushi is 3 pieces (approximately 90g), each piece consisting of a hand-formed oval of vinegared sushi rice topped with a slice of raw salmon.

nutrient_source.title: Salmon Sushi — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for Salmon Sushi (3pcs). Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is set to -1. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salmon nigiri sushi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated based on the GI of sushi rice (white short-grain rice prepared with vinegar), which is reported at approximately 55 in Atkinson et al. (2008) and Brand-Miller et al. peer-reviewed publications. Vinegar in sushi rice is documented to modestly lower the glycaemic response of white rice. The salmon topping contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially affect GI. GL per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (18.39g/100g × 90g serving = 16.55g) / 100 = 9.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 428mg/100g with low potassium offset (48mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit salmon sushi intake and strictly avoid soy sauce dipping, as the combined sodium load from sushi rice seasoning and soy sauce can substantially exceed recommended daily sodium limits; low-sodium soy sauce and minimal pickled ginger use are advised if consumed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined white sushi rice contributes 18.4g carbohydrate/100g with only 1.0g dietary fibre, producing a moderate-to-high glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should moderate portion size of salmon sushi due to the refined white rice base; requesting brown rice sushi where available and pairing with non-starchy vegetables can help attenuate the postprandial glucose response, while the salmon component itself provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (428mg/100g) is a cardiovascular risk factor, partially offset by the cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids in raw salmon
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the raw salmon in sushi provides EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids that support heart health, but the high sodium content from seasoned sushi rice and typical soy sauce accompaniment poses a meaningful cardiovascular risk; consumption should be moderate and soy sauce use minimised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 from raw salmon partially offset by high sodium promoting tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw salmon is an excellent source of anti-inflammatory EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis, but the 428mg/100g sodium content may exacerbate joint swelling; salmon sashimi (without rice) is a more RA-suitable alternative that retains the omega-3 benefit without the sodium and glycaemic load of sushi rice.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salmon sushi (サーモン寿司) is a Japanese dish consisting of hand-formed ovals of vinegared short-grain white rice topped with thin slices of fresh raw Atlantic or Pacific salmon. A standard serving is 3 pieces (approximately 90g). The rice is seasoned with a mixture of rice vinegar, sugar, and salt, giving it a subtly tangy and slightly sweet flavour that complements the rich, buttery texture of raw salmon. The salmon topping is soft, silky, and mildly fatty with a clean oceanic flavour. Salmon sushi is widely consumed in Japan and internationally, including in Singapore where it is a popular item at Japanese restaurants and supermarket sushi counters. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 94 kcal, 2.92g protein, 0.67g fat, and 18.39g carbohydrate, with sodium at 428mg — the dominant nutritional concern. The carbohydrate content is primarily from refined white sushi rice, which contributes a moderate glycaemic load. The salmon component provides omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) with well-documented cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory benefits. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients, particularly when soy sauce is used as a dipping condiment. For type 2 diabetes management, the refined rice base requires portion control. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans and contains fish allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in 3 pieces of salmon sushi?
Is salmon sushi high in sodium?
What is the GI of sushi?
Is salmon sushi good for heart health?
How much protein is in salmon sushi?
Can I eat salmon sushi if I have high blood pressure?
What are the omega-3 benefits of salmon sushi?

confidence_reason: All per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for Salmon Sushi with review_status reviewed and confidence 0.95. The HPB is an approved primary regional source. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved GI source (sushi rice GI from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008) and is clearly documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: Saturated_fat_g set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in HPB Foodo for this dish. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond the HPB-reported fields not available in the HPB Foodo database for this entry as of 2026-04-16. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sushi rice (white short-grain rice with vinegar) from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. (2008); no direct GI measurement for salmon nigiri sushi exists in the approved GI database. Halal status is not confirmed as sushi rice seasoning and accompaniments (mirin, sake) may contain alcohol; is_halal set to false by default. The description references 3pcs as per the input record description.
